narrative: Dear [Student's Name], as a Black woman applying to law school, you've shown determination in pursuing this path, and we appreciate the courage it takes given the unique challenges many face in higher education. Your profile includes a solid middle family income background, full-time study plans, an undergraduate GPA of 2.70—which is a bit below the typical 3.21 average—and an LSAT score of 20, notably lower than the dataset average of around 37. In your school's grading, you ranked in the 5th decile for Year 1 performance, close to average, but dropped to the 1st decile in Year 3, far below the usual 5th or 6th decile mark. The admissions model, which predicts first-time bar exam success based on patterns from thousands of similar students, estimated an 84% chance you'll face failure on that critical test. This high predicted risk directly led to your application's rejection, as the school prioritizes candidates likely to pass and thrive in practice. The biggest factor pulling the prediction toward failure was your Year 3 school decile ranking, which stood out as a strong warning sign compared to peers who maintained higher standings. Your LSAT score was the next major influence in the same direction, signaling to the model a tougher road ahead relative to typical admits. On a brighter note, your Year 1 decile helped push slightly toward a passing prediction, as did your undergraduate GPA, both showing some foundational promise above the lowest thresholds. Your middle income and full-time commitment had minimal sway but didn't hurt your case. Compared to students who typically get approved, your later academic dip and LSAT were the starkest departures from their stronger, more consistent profiles. To move forward, consider retaking the LSAT to boost that score, seeking targeted tutoring for advanced coursework to improve your decile rankings, and exploring programs with bar prep support tailored for underrepresented students like yourself. You're capable of turning this around—many have—and we encourage reapplying after strengthening these areas. Keep pushing; your story matters in law.
